pages.title: Discharge Planning Process
pages.content:
Discharge Planning Process

Effective discharge planning begins at admission. Follow this structured process to ensure safe, timely patient transitions.

Active Synthetic Care-Manager Cases

  • Jennifer Moore — care manager: Dr. Donald Wilson; latest interaction: morning rounding note and medication follow-up message.
  • Margaret Hernandez — care manager: Dr. Mary Gonzalez; latest interaction: discharge readiness note and post-discharge outreach message.

Process Steps

  1. Admission Assessment — Identify anticipated discharge needs within 24 hours of admission
  2. Interdisciplinary Planning — Coordinate with pharmacy, social work, PT/OT, and case management
  3. Patient Education — Verify teach-back on medications, warning signs, and follow-up plan
  4. Discharge Order — Physician completes discharge summary and medication reconciliation
  5. Post-Discharge Follow-up — Care coordinator confirms follow-up within 48 hours

Key Metrics

  • Target discharge turnaround: 4 hours or less from order to departure
  • 30-day readmission target: 12% or less
  • Follow-up appointment compliance: 95% or more

pages.title: Escalation Procedures
pages.content:
Clinical Escalation Procedures

Use this reference for escalating patient care, operational, and safety concerns through the proper chain of command.

Clinical Escalation Levels

LevelPathResponse Time
1Charge Nurse → Nurse Manager15 minutes
2Nurse Manager → Department Director30 minutes
3Department Director → CMO / CNO1 hour

Rapid Response

Any staff member may call a Rapid Response for acute patient deterioration. Dial extension 4444. Do not wait for chain-of-command approval.

messages: DMC meeting is March 15. I need the unblinded interim dataset locked by March 8. Biostatistics — can you confirm the analysis timeline?
Confirmed. Statistical analysis plan is finalized. SAP v3.0 was signed off last week. Dataset lock by March 8 is feasible if all queries are resolved by March 5.
CRO confirmed 98% query resolution as of today. I'll push for 100% by March 3 to give us buffer. Any concerns from the medical monitor?
No safety concerns flagged. The latest SUSAR report was reviewed and determined unrelated to study drug. We're clear to proceed with the interim.
Great. I'll send the DMC logistics package today — meeting agenda, charter reminder, and data access credentials for the secure portal.
Quick question — the FDA reviewer asked for additional stability data for the DEM-027 NDA. Do we have the 24-month data available yet?
Yes, 24-month data just came in last week. All timepoints within specification. I'll compile the stability tables and updated shelf-life justification by Thursday.
Perfect. I'll prepare the information request response letter. Can you also include the updated certificate of analysis for the 24-month pull?
Will do. I'll package everything with the batch records and send for your review before we submit the IR response.
HPLC-04 in Lab R2 failed the system suitability test this morning. Resolution is out of spec — looks like the column might be degraded.
I'll check the column usage log. That column has 1,200 injections on it. We should have a backup C18 column in storage. Let me grab it.
Column swapped and SST passed. Resolution is 2.8 (spec ≥ 2.0). I've logged the column change in the equipment maintenance system.
Thanks for the quick turnaround. Remember to add the old column to the waste log and decommission it in the inventory system.

pages.title: Drug Development Process
pages.content:
Drug Development Process

Our standardized development framework from target identification through regulatory approval.

Stage Gates

  1. Discovery — Target ID, hit-to-lead, lead optimization
  2. Preclinical — ADME, toxicology, GLP studies, formulation
  3. IND Filing — CMC, nonclinical summary, protocol, investigator brochure
  4. Phase I — First-in-human (FIH), dose escalation, PK/PD
  5. Phase II — Dose-finding, preliminary efficacy, safety expansion
  6. Phase III — Pivotal efficacy, global enrollment, regulatory endpoints
  7. NDA/BLA — Submission, FDA review, advisory committee, approval

pages.title: GLP Compliance Guidelines
pages.content:
GLP Compliance Guidelines

Good Laboratory Practice compliance is required for all nonclinical safety studies submitted to regulatory agencies.

Key Requirements

  • Study Director must be identified before study initiation
  • All equipment calibrated and documented per SOPs
  • Raw data maintained in laboratory notebooks (electronic or paper)
  • Quality Assurance unit must audit each study phase
  • Specimens retained per protocol-specified retention schedule

Recent Inspections

Last FDA GLP inspection: Q4 2025 — No findings

Next inspection window: Q3 2026

documents.content:
STATISTICAL ANALYSIS PLAN — DEM-042-301 v3.0
Contoso Pharmaceuticals (Synthetic)

PRIMARY ANALYSIS
  Endpoint: Progression-free survival (PFS)
  Method: Stratified log-rank test (stratification: ECOG, region)
  Alpha: 0.05 (two-sided), with O'Brien-Fleming spending function for interim

SAMPLE SIZE
  N = 500 (2:1 randomization)
  Power: 90% to detect HR = 0.65
  Expected median PFS control: 6.0 months

INTERIM ANALYSIS
  Planned at 60% information fraction (~200 events)
  DMC review: March 15, 2026
  Stopping boundaries: Efficacy (p < 0.005) or futility (conditional power < 20%)

SECONDARY ANALYSES
  - Overall survival (OS): Stratified log-rank, final analysis at 400 events
  - Objective response rate (ORR): Exact binomial CI
  - Duration of response (DOR): Kaplan-Meier

PQ protocol for the new laser welding station starts Monday. We need 3 consecutive lots of 100 units each. Production has confirmed line time.
I've set up the inspection plan — every unit gets visual inspection, 10% destructive tensile testing, 100% leak testing. Acceptance criteria are in the protocol.

Good call. I'll also pull the complaint trend data for the last 12 months to see if this is truly a new signal or an ongoing low-level issue.

pages.title: Design Control Process
pages.content:
Design Control Process (per 21 CFR 820.30)

Our stage-gate design control process ensures each medical device meets user needs, intended use, and regulatory requirements.

Phases

  1. Design Input — User needs, design requirements, regulatory requirements, risk-based requirements
  2. Design Output — Drawings, specifications, software, labelling, BOM
  3. Design Review — Cross-functional formal review at each phase gate
  4. Design Verification — Confirm outputs meet inputs (bench testing, analysis)
  5. Design Validation — Confirm device meets user needs (simulated use, clinical)
  6. Design Transfer — Release to manufacturing with validated processes

Key Documents

  • Design History File (DHF)
  • Device Master Record (DMR)
  • Device History Record (DHR)

pages.title: Risk Management Framework
pages.content:
Risk Management — ISO 14971

All Contoso Medical Devices products follow a risk management process compliant with ISO 14971:2019.

Process Steps

  1. Risk Analysis — Identify hazards, hazardous situations, and harms (DFMEA + use FMEA)
  2. Risk Evaluation — Compare estimated risk against acceptability criteria
  3. Risk Control — Implement mitigation measures (design, protective, information)
  4. Residual Risk Assessment — Verify overall residual risk is acceptable
  5. Production & Post-Production — Monitor field performance and update risk file

Risk Acceptability Matrix

Severity × Probability matrix with defined thresholds per product class (Class I, II, III)

posts: Welcome to Claims Operations. Post adjudication metrics updates, workflow changes, and escalation questions here.
Auto-adjudication rate hit 78% this week — up 3 points from January. Top denial reason remains missing prior authorization (code 4401). Working with Provider Relations to address.
New claim edit rule deployed: CMS-1500 claims with invalid NPI will auto-pend instead of denying. This should reduce provider abrasion by ~200 claims/week.
Reminder: Claims inventory target is 5 days or less. Current average is 4.8 days. Let's keep the momentum — check your pended queues daily.
System maintenance this Saturday 02:00–06:00 EST. Claims processing will be paused. All batch runs will resume automatically after maintenance.
Q1 first-pass resolution rate: 82% (target 85%). Root cause analysis shows 60% of rework is from incomplete provider submissions. Provider education campaign launching next month.

teams_channels.description: Member experience updates, enrollment reporting, grievance tracking, and satisfaction surveys
posts: January member satisfaction survey results: Overall satisfaction 4.1/5.0 (target 4.0). Call center average hold time: 2.5 minutes (target ≤ 3.0).
Open enrollment recap: 12,400 new member enrollments processed. 98.5% of ID cards shipped within the 10-day SLA. Great work by the enrollment team.
Grievance trending: 45 grievances received in January (down 12% from December). Top category: claim denial disputes (40%). G&A team is meeting weekly to address.
New member portal features launched: Online ID card replacement, digital EOB access, and provider directory search. Member training webinar is next Tuesday.
Star Ratings focus: HEDIS measure gaps report distributed to care management team. Diabetic eye exam compliance is at 62% — our Q4 target is 75%.

teams_channels.description: Provider credentialing, network adequacy reporting, contract negotiations, and provider communications
posts: Network adequacy report — Q1 2026: All 14 counties meet CMS time-and-distance standards for PCP, behavioral health, and specialist access. One county on watch for OB/GYN.
Credentialing backlog update: 34 applications pending (15 are re-credentialing). Average processing time: 18 days (target ≤ 30 days). Clean pass rate: 91%.
Provider contract renewal cycle starts next month. Templates for Tier 1 (hospital systems) and Tier 2 (large group practices) are updated and approved by legal.
New network addition: Lakewood Orthopedic Associates (5 providers) joined the network effective March 1. Directory update has been pushed to the portal.
Provider satisfaction survey results: 3.8/5.0 overall. Biggest pain point: claims payment turnaround. We're targeting 14-day clean claim payment by Q3.

teams_channels.description: CMS/state regulatory compliance, market conduct exam preparation, and internal audit updates
posts: CMS Program Audit notification received for Q3 2026. Scope: Part D formulary compliance, coverage determinations, and appeals/grievances. Prep meetings start in April.
State Department of Insurance market conduct exam concluded with no significant findings. Final report expected in 30 days.
Quarterly compliance training completion: 94% (target 100%). 38 employees have overdue modules — list sent to department managers.
FWA (Fraud, Waste, and Abuse) tip line received 12 reports in January. 8 investigated and closed (no substantiated fraud). 4 in active investigation.

messages: Flagging a batch of 42 claims stuck in pended status for 8+ days — all from Lakewood Medical Group. Looks like a systematic NPI mapping issue post-contract update.
I see the issue. Their new Tax ID wasn't linked to the existing provider group record. I'll update the provider master file and reprocess the batch today.
Thanks. I've notified their practice manager about the delay. Can you also check if any of their members had EOBs generated with incorrect denial language?
Checked — 6 EOBs went out with denial code 197 (invalid provider). I'll flag those for corrected EOBs and send an apology letter template to Member Services.
Good. Let's also add a QA check for provider master file updates going forward. This shouldn't have slipped through without validation.
HEDIS gap report shows we're behind on diabetic eye exam (DEE) measure — 62% versus our Q4 target of 75%. That's 1,400 open gaps across our commercial book.
I'll work with the care management team to launch targeted outreach. Can we get a list of members who had a PCP visit in the last 90 days but no DEE on file?
Yes — I'll pull the claims-based gap list by Friday. We should also check if any gaps are due to missing supplemental data from provider EMR feeds.
Good point. I'll coordinate with Health Informatics to run the supplemental data reconciliation. Some of those exams may have been done but not captured in our system.
CMS 834 enrollment file for February has a 0.3% delta versus our internal enrollment count. 47 members show active in CMS but termed in our system.
That's within the reporting tolerance, but we should reconcile. Most likely a timing issue with retroactive terminations processed after the file cutoff.
I'll pull the list and cross-check against our term transactions. If any are billing-active members, we need to alert Premium Billing before the next invoice cycle.
Found the root cause — 39 of 47 are COBRA continuations that our system termed at initial separation but CMS has as active. The COBRA election notices went out late.
I'll update the enrollment records and submit a corrected 834 by Friday. Going forward, COBRA elections should be flagged before the outgoing enrollment file is generated.
Preliminary Q1 MLR (Medical Loss Ratio) is coming in at 86.2% for the commercial book. Target is 85%. The variance is driven by a spike in high-cost claimants in January.
How many high-cost claimants exceeded the $100K threshold? If it's reinsurance-eligible costs we can net those out of the MLR numerator.
14 members exceeded $100K. Aggregate stop-loss kicks in at $1.2M — we're at $980K, so likely won't trigger this quarter. Individual spec is covering 9 of the 14.
Let's model the run-rate for Q2. If the high-cost trend continues, we may need to revisit the pharmacy benefit carve-out strategy. I'll have projections by Wednesday.

pages.title: Claims Adjudication Workflow
pages.content:
Claims Adjudication Workflow

End-to-end claims processing from receipt through payment or denial.

Process Steps

  1. Receipt & Registration — EDI 837 or paper claim received, registered in claims system
  2. Front-End Edits — Eligibility, duplicate check, provider status, timely filing
  3. Auto-Adjudication — Rules engine applies benefit design, fee schedule, medical policy
  4. Pend & Review — Claims not auto-adjudicated route to examiner queue by complexity
  5. Determination — Pay, deny, or pend for additional information
  6. Payment/EOB — EFT/check issued, EOB generated to member and provider

SLA Targets

  • Clean claims: 30 calendar days (contractual)
  • Internal target: 14 days
  • Appeals: 30 days (standard), 72 hours (expedited)

documents.content:
CLAIMS PROCESSING STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURES
Contoso Health Plans (Synthetic)

1. SCOPE
This SOP covers professional (CMS-1500), institutional (UB-04), and pharmacy claims processing.

2. CLAIM RECEIPT
  - EDI: 837P/837I transactions processed within 2 hours of receipt
  - Paper: Scanned and indexed within 24 hours, OCR data entry within 48 hours
  - Portal: Provider-submitted claims available in real-time

3. FRONT-END EDITS
  - Member eligibility verified against enrollment database
  - Provider status validated (active, credentialed, in-network)
  - Duplicate claim detection (same member, provider, date, CPT)
  - Timely filing check (180 days for in-network, 365 for out-of-network)

4. AUTO-ADJUDICATION
  - Fee schedule pricing (contracted rate or Medicare allowable)
  - Benefit accumulator check (deductible, copay, coinsurance, OOP max)
  - Medical policy edits (prior auth, medical necessity, bundling)
  - Coordination of benefits (COB) if applicable

5. MANUAL REVIEW
  - Complex claim types: hospital inpatient, out-of-network, high-dollar
  - Clinical review threshold: $25,000 (auto-route to medical director)
